title: Decoding Random Latent Vectors
concept: Passing a randomly sampled latent vector through the trained decoder produces a brand new, plausible output that was never in the training set.

exercises.prompt: When you scribble a totally random doodle, is it ever something new that didn't exist before?

options.text: Yes, a random doodle can be a brand-new picture
No, doodles must copy something already drawn
Only if you trace it exactly

exercises.explain: Right! Randomness can create something brand new — that's exactly what happens when we feed random numbers into a trained decoder.

cards.title: What Happens If We Roll the Dice?
cards.body: Imagine a machine that learned to draw faces. Now what if you feed it a totally random list of numbers instead of a real face's numbers? Surprise — it still draws a face! Just one that's never existed before. That's the magic we're exploring today.

cards.title: Decoding a Random Latent Vector
cards.body: A 'latent vector' is a short list of numbers that captures the hidden 'recipe' of something (like a face or a drawing). When we randomly pick numbers for this list and feed them into a trained decoder, it turns that recipe into a full, realistic output — a brand new example that was never actually in the training data.

cards.title: The Magic Recipe Machine
cards.body: Think of the decoder as a cookie machine that turns recipe cards into cookies. During training, it learned from thousands of real recipe cards. Now, if you hand it a brand-new recipe card with random ingredient amounts (that follow the general pattern it learned), it still bakes a real, edible cookie — just one nobody has ever tasted before!

cards.title: Generating a New Face
cards.body: A model trained on thousands of face photos learns a latent space where each point is a set of numbers. If we randomly sample a new point — say [0.3, -1.2, 0.8, ...] — and pass it through the decoder, it outputs a realistic face image. That exact face was never in the training photos, but it looks just as real.

cards.title: Myth: It's Just Copying a Training Example
cards.body: Nope! People often think the decoder is secretly picking a photo it memorized. But it's actually blending and generating patterns it learned, creating something original. That's why generative models can make endless new, unique outputs instead of just replaying old ones.

cards.title: Real Photo vs. Decoded Random Vector
cards.body: One comes straight from a camera. The other comes from numbers turned into an image by the decoder.

left.text: An actual picture that existed before training — captured directly from the real world.

right.text: A brand-new image created by feeding random numbers into the trained decoder — plausible but never seen before.

exercises.prompt: What happens when you pass a randomly sampled latent vector through a trained decoder?

options.text: It always returns an exact training example
It produces a new, plausible output not in the training set
It crashes because the vector isn't real data
It returns a blank or empty output

exercises.explain: The decoder generalizes what it learned, turning random numbers into a realistic new output that wasn't part of training.

exercises.prompt: A decoded random latent vector always matches one of the exact images used during training.
exercises.explain: False! The decoder generates new, original outputs based on learned patterns — it doesn't just replay memorized training examples.

exercises.sentence: Feeding a randomly sampled latent vector into a trained decoder creates a brand new, ___ output.
exercises.explain: 'Plausible' means it looks realistic and believable, even though it's a completely new creation, not a copy.

exercises.prompt: An AI art app lets you click 'Randomize' to generate a totally new character. Which statement best explains what's happening under the hood?

options.text: A random latent vector is sampled and decoded into a new, plausible character image
The app picks a random photo from its training folder and shows it
The app deletes and redraws the same character each time

exercises.explain: Correct! 'Randomize' typically samples a new latent vector and runs it through the decoder to generate a fresh, original output.

exercises.prompt: You built a decoder trained on thousands of cat photos. You feed it a random latent vector it has never used before. What should you expect?

options.text: An error, since the vector wasn't part of training
A brand-new, realistic-looking cat image that never existed before
One of the exact training photos, picked at random
A random collection of unrelated pixels with no pattern

exercises.explain: A well-trained decoder turns any reasonable latent vector into a plausible output — here, a brand-new, realistic cat image that was never actually in the training data.
